mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, make it this: it's a monster A I day. Google's Gemini three point five Pro goes live for everyone, right as open models have their best week ever.

Section one. A I developments.

Google's Gemini three point five Pro hits general availability today. It was rebuilt on a fresh pretraining run rather than adapted from the old model, and it ships a two million token context window, double anything else at the frontier. The extended reasoning mode, called Deep Think, sits behind the two hundred fifty dollar a month Ultra subscription.

Meanwhile, from your A I News reads, it has been a huge week for open models. Moonshot released Kimi K three, a two point eight trillion parameter model that is the largest open model ever released. It performs at the level of top closed models, at a fraction of the price. And Thinking Machines, Mira Murati's lab, released Inkling, a nine hundred seventy five billion parameter multimodal model that is now the best American open model with a fully permissive license. Your Axios A I plus newsletter adds that Inkling deliberately trades raw power for flexibility and lower cost, so businesses can customize it for their own needs. The chief executive of Hugging Face says enterprises increasingly want open models, and that the real A I race may no longer be at the frontier.

One more, from your Rundown: Open A I made its hardware debut. It's called the Codex Micro, a two hundred thirty dollar limited run light up keyboard for commanding fleets of coding agents. It has keys that show agent status, and a dial that adjusts how much reasoning an agent uses. A screenless smart speaker is reportedly coming next year.

Why it matters: the gap between closed frontier models and open ones is narrowing fast, and pricing power is shifting toward users.

Section two. A I ethics and policy.

The Federal Trade Commission opened public comment on a policy statement about A I accuracy, with comments due July thirty first. In Illinois, the governor signed a new A I safety law requiring model developers to publish frameworks for assessing catastrophic risk. States have now passed over one hundred A I laws this year. Across the Atlantic, the European Commission presented an action plan that fuses cybersecurity and A I regulation, and so called nudify apps are set to be banned across the E U from December.

And from your Axios A I plus in Readwise, a piece they call the A I pocalypse jobs board: Anthropic is hiring experts to prevent A I from being misused for dangerous weapons, while a new report finds Google Search's A I features give children unsafe or incorrect answers. The through line: companies are leading on safety while government rules lag behind.

Why it matters: a patchwork of state, federal, and European rules is hardening right now, and compliance and communications teams will feel it first.

Section three. Chips and geopolitics.

The U S Commerce Department affirmed that the A I chip export ban applies to Chinese firms' subsidiaries outside China, closing the offshore loophole. Prosecutors also charged three men with smuggling two and a half billion dollars' worth of servers packed with restricted Nvidia chips to Chinese buyers. At least five hundred ten million dollars of hardware got through. The backdrop: a global chip ecosystem splitting in two, with some Nvidia sales to China allowed, but only under strict volume caps and independent testing.

Why it matters: chips remain the choke point of A I competition. Enforcement is tightening even as some sales reopen.

Section four. Media watch, from your Status newsletters.

The New York Times is locked in a subpoena standoff. Elon Musk's deepfakes lawsuit is moving ahead. M S NOW faces a pivotal moment, and there are fresh questions about Netflix's strategy. Status also just held its first summit.

Why it matters: press freedom fights and A I deepfakes are converging on the media industry's turf. That's your world.

Section five. Tech for good, and some good news.

Vint Cerf, one of the fathers of the internet, is building a standard to identify A I agents on the open web. Think of it as the plumbing for trustworthy agent traffic. From your Noosphere newsletter: Brazilian federal prosecutors filed an emergency lawsuit against Axia Energia over a health crisis affecting the Parakana people, who say they are drinking mud. Accountability in action. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
